RULING:
PLAINTIFFS' MOTION TO COMPEL THE CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION TO PRODUCE A PERSON MOST KNOWLEDGEABLE AND CUSTODIAN OF RECORDS AND TO PRODUCE DOCUMENTS

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Plaintiffs' motion to compel the California Department of Transportation to produce a person most knowledgeable and custodian of records and to produce documents is denied as untimely. The motion was made more than 60 days after the completion of the record, in violation of C.C.P. 2025.480(b). (See Unzipped Apparel, LLC v. Bader (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 123.) Prevailing party is to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Garrett L. Wong, Reporter: Claudia J. Knap of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #6099 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/GLW)

The February 6, 2017 trial date is maintained with preference status pursuant to C.C.P. 36(a). Last day pursuant to C.C.P. 36(f) is February 8, 2017. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar May-06-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Dean Pollack of BURNHAM BROWN (510-835-6714), appearing for defendant / moving party BorgWarner Morse Tec Inc. as Successor-by-Merger to Borg-Warner Corporation
James P. Nevin of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Theodore Winter and Janet Winter

RULING:
DEFENDANT BORGWARNER MORSE TEC INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant BorgWarner Morse TEC Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication of plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages is granted. As to summary judgment, defendant failed to sustain its initial burden under either prong of Aguilar. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that Mr. Winter was exposed to a product attributable to defendant. Plaintiff Theodore Winter's identification during his deposition of a BorgWarner clutch is admissible. (In re Cindy L. (1997) 17 Cal.4th 15.) The deposition testimony of Theodore Winter and Brad Winter preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. Defendant also failed to sustain its burden of negating an essential element of plaintiffs' claims. Even assuming the declaration of Mr. Merz is admissible, it merely creates a triable issue when read in conjunction with Brad Winter's deposition testimony and it has no bearing on Theodore Winter's deposition testimony. For summary judgment, product identification was the only argument made by defendant. As to summary adjudication of plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages, plaintiffs failed to present clear and convincing evidence creating a triable issue whether defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. While plaintiffs do not need to prove their case for punitive damages at the summary adjudication stage, the Court takes the higher proof standard into consideration. (See American Airlines, Inc. v. Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 1017; Hoch v. Allied-Signal, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 48.) Plaintiffs' objections to Mr. Merz's declaration are overruled. Plaintiffs' objections to Richard Anderson's declaration are overruled. Defendant's objections to Brad Winter's deposition testimony are overruled. Defendant's objections to Plaintiffs' Exhibit J (1997 responses to standard interrogatories) are sustained. As discussed at the April 28, 2015 hearing, defendant is awarded the costs it incurred in drafting an additional reply due to plaintiffs' failure to file a timely opposition. That amount is $1,755.00. Order signed in open court. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Desiree Winn of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #13645 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)
